summary: Fowler and colleague Mike Mason experimented with Stable Diffusion to generate portraits of 'technical authors,' finding the results revealing about AI biases. The initial images (implied to show predominantly white males) prompted reflection on how AI training data reflects societal biases. When ChatGPT was asked to generate better prompts, it notably included one specifically describing a 'confident female technical writer' - suggesting the AI recognized the need to explicitly specify diversity. Fowler finds it telling that ChatGPT chose to include gendered specifications in its suggestions, highlighting how default assumptions get encoded into AI systems.
key_points: Simple prompts to image-generating AI reveal embedded biases about who technical authors are
ChatGPT's prompt suggestions included explicit gender specification for diversity, implying the default assumption is male
The experiment reflects not just AI limitations but broader societal biases captured in training data
More sophisticated prompts might produce different results, but the baseline assumptions remain revealing
money_quotes: The results speak volumes about the current state of AI. And perhaps of our broader society - at least as the internet sees it.
I was intrigued by what ChatGPT chose to put in its prompts.
A confident female technical writer, wearing a blazer and a headset, working on a tablet with a stylus
key_insight: What AI generates from simple prompts reveals the biases embedded in its training data - and by extension, the biases present in how our society represents certain professions online.
